paragraphs.body: In the late 1990s a couple decided that in order to give the best future to their future children, they needed to pack their bags and leave good old Puebla, Mexico. Leaving behind everything they ever knew and the people that were dear to them too. Their overall destination was the United States of America in order to chase that American dream. That couple just happens to be my parents. My parents, who left their childhood homes, their friends, and the culture that was all around them. But most of all a place that they knew so much about.instead They headed to Philadelphia with their luggage in hand that contained their broken English, a few dollars, and pieces of their Mexican culture that they were able to cling to, and with hopes that would be enough for just now. With no doubt in mind, they were able to build a home and build a flourishing family. They were living that dream they had envisioned themselves in.

paragraphs.body: My ‘home’ language is the language that I speak with my mother and father at home. My family that they built. My community. “¿Ya terminaste con tus clases?” (You're done with your classes?) “¿Ya comiste?” (Did you eat yet?) are phrases that flood my ear when I put my phone against my ear every time I call my mom. Pestering about my well-being, “¿Qué estás haciendo? (What are you doing?), is what I flood her ears with. I'm always so nosy to know what she's been up to, even being 305 miles away. “¿Qué vas a cocinar hoy?” (What are you going to cook today?) I always have to ask; I'm curious what the menu of the day will be—the food that will be filling up my family's bellies and not mine. Mole, pozole, enchiladas, albóndigas, tostadas, caldo de pollo, etc. Mexican dishes I eat and enjoy. What reminds me of home. These dishes not only tie me with my family but also tie me with my Mexican community. I speak Spanish to the customers that come into my dad's restaurant. “¡Hola! ¿En qué te puedo ayudar hoy?” I welcome them and start reciting this week's special “birra tacos” and all the meats we have in Spanish. ”Carne de res, carnitas, al pastor, birria, chorizo." With Spanish being the language I spoke back at home and to my parents, there was never a peak of any foreign language or any unknown territory crossed.

paragraphs.body: My whole world turned upside down in my freshman year of college. I entered foreign territory. Instead of going into a whole new country, that unknown territory was just 6 hours away from home. I thought I knew everything, but really I didn't. Spending the first few days at college, where I'll be living, I was confused. I recall not knowing how to use my student ID to open my dorm door. I didn't know how to pay for a washer in order to do a load of laundry. I didn't really understand how the meal plan really worked; I was confused with using meal swipes and dining dollars. I even question if I really knew if I'm even majoring in the right major. Entering college means integrating with the college language—the language I speak with everyone here, the professors and my peers. “Research,” “internships”, “loans”, ”lecture and recitation”, “credits,” and “FAFSA,” even if I'm confused on what everything means. Everyone around seems to have a better grip and understanding than me, but I try not to get discouraged. Being the first person in my family to go to college, this is the language I speak away from my family at home. Half the time they mostly don't understand the words coming out of my mouth regarding college, so I do explain. I myself am also searching for explanations. Just like many other first-gen students who are also integrating themselves into the college world. We are mostly confused and weighed down with many questions to ask. “Echale ganas (give it your all) is a saying I heard often growing up. The two words that I always received whenever I informed my parents of any upcoming exams I had to take, whether it was the PSSAs or the SAT. These were the two words that my dad said to me before leaving after dropping me at the entrance of my dorm building. It probably weighs a few grams, but this phrase weighs pounds in my heart. To me, it means resilience and effort, to keep on going and giving. This universal phrase has been said by many Hispanic parents, but it's very meaningful, at least to me. Growing up, my parents were not involved in my academic life. They didn't pester me about my grades. They didn't know about the important exams I had to take. They didn't know about my dreams and aspirations. There was distance between us. Distance that needed fixing. With college being away from home, the distance was still large. I never find myself telling them What's weighing me down are my worries and hopes, what needs to get done. “They just don't understand” is what I tell myself, but I know they support me because of the saying they always say to me, which I use to heartily encourage myself to keep on going even when I'm unsure of myself half of the time.

Just like many other first-gen students who are also integrating themselves into the college world. We are mostly confused and weighed down with many questions to ask.
It probably weighs a few grams, but this phrase weighs pounds in my heart.

paragraphs.body: To live in between languages means knowing To rearrange our words and serve it to whoever orders it To live a life your parents don't know Loving two dishes, but they're from different cultures So you eat one dish at a time; So the two dishes won't touch. But you did wish they tasted good when mushed together.

annotations.text: So you eat one dish at a time;

paragraphs.body: Although my parents and I are involved in a different language, we do have some common ground. We both strive for a better future. They left their country for their family, and I left home for a greater education. So maybe we both understand each other better than we thought.

annotations.comment: I like the move to echo your intro paragraph here, but I wonder if you could develop and extend this conclusion to better address the important (and nuanced) ideas at the heart of your essay?
